Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd get distracted or just not feel like it. I'm a visual learner, so I'd sometimes spend hours watching videos or making colorful notes, but that didn't happen on any real schedule. Most weeks I'd cram everything into random bursts when I remembered I had something due. I know 16 hours sounds like decent study time, but it was scattered all over the place.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was going on. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd panic and try to catch up. Deadlines were the main thing that got me moving, but even then I'd often miss them. I never really established a consistent rhythm—it just felt too hard to stick to anything with everything else happening in my life.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that made me log in. Like I'd suddenly remember I hadn't checked in for days and wonder what I'd missed. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline and that would push me to at least look at what was happening. Other times I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I didn't actually do much once I was there.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit to check in every morning, but that fell apart pretty quickly. The LMS never felt like something I naturally wanted to open—it was always more of a chore. I'd have to force myself, and that kind of routine just didn't stick for me.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me since I learn better visually. I watched a lot of the lecture videos, even if I didn't always engage with the other stuff. When there were diagrams or visual explanations, things clicked better for me. The videos felt less overwhelming than reading through walls of text, so I gravitated toward them when I actually did study.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion boards and reading materials. The discussions felt awkward—I never knew what to say and it seemed like other people had it more together than me. The readings were dense and I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never really came. I also barely touched the practice quizzes because they stressed me out too much.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it was visual and engaging. If something was going to take forever and I wasn't sure it would help my grade, I'd skip it. I needed to feel like there was a point to what I was doing, otherwise my brain just checked out. Quick wins felt more motivating than long, complicated tasks.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they didn't matter, honestly. When something was required, I'd at least attempt it, even if I submitted it late or incomplete. But optional stuff? My brain just categorized it as 'not important' and I'd never get around to it. It's like I needed the external pressure of a deadline or grade to make myself do anything.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos and study from those on my tablet without being connected. I also used YouTube a lot to find explanations of concepts that confused me—sometimes random creators explained things better than the course materials. My notes were all handwritten and color-coded, which helped me remember things visually but obviously that doesn't show up anywhere online.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours going through my handwritten notes or rewatching downloaded videos, and none of that would register on the LMS. I'd also study with a friend occasionally, going over concepts together, but we weren't doing it through any official platform. Some of my best understanding came from those offline sessions that left no digital trace.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download a video or PDF once and then use it repeatedly offline. It was easier to study on my tablet without internet distractions. So the LMS might show I accessed something once, but I probably reviewed it multiple times on my own. That's just how I preferred to work—less cluttered, fewer notifications pulling me away.
Q12: Around midterms things got really hard. My anxiety was through the roof and I was dealing with some personal stuff that made it hard to focus. I'd sit down to study and just feel paralyzed, like everything was too much. The CBT sessions helped a bit with managing those feelings, but there were definitely weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage at all.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or tired, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Having five online courses was overwhelming—there was always something due somewhere and I couldn't keep track. Some days I was just too exhausted to even log in, let alone actually learn anything. The fatigue made everything feel impossible.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd log in and scroll through stuff without really absorbing anything. Like I was going through the motions but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch videos but realize at the end that I hadn't retained a single thing. It was frustrating because I was technically 'studying' but getting nothing out of it.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. The anxiety made it worse—thinking about the work made me so stressed that I'd avoid it to feel better in the moment. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. It's a terrible cycle but I couldn't seem to break out of it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me do anything. I'd ignore stuff until it was almost too late, then scramble to submit something. But even then, I missed a lot of deadlines because I'd misjudge how long things would take or just freeze up. The pressure helped sometimes but other times it just made my anxiety worse and I'd shut down completely.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got encouraging feedback, it motivated me for like a day, but then I'd slip back into old patterns. Negative feedback just made me want to avoid the course more. I think I needed more consistent, gentle feedback to stay on track, but by the time I got comments back I'd already mentally moved on.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so many components to keep track of. Having five online courses meant I was constantly juggling different platforms and deadlines. If things had been more streamlined or if there were clearer weekly checklists, maybe I would've stayed more on top of things. The flexibility that's supposed to help actually made it easier for me to drift.
Q19: The lack of structure made engagement harder for me. I needed more guidance on what to prioritize each week. The videos being available was great for my learning style, but having so many resources without clear direction on what was essential versus optional left me paralyzed. Simpler, more visual course layouts would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think that's totally me, actually. I'd log in, watch videos, click around, but then when it came to actually submitting work, I'd freeze up. The anxiety of being judged on my work was different from just passively consuming content. Being active in the LMS felt safer than putting myself out there with assignments that would be graded.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone studies mostly offline with textbooks or downloaded materials, their LMS activity would be low but they could still learn the content. That wasn't really me though—my low activity matched my poor performance. But I know people who barely logged in and did fine because they had other ways of studying.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. They miss all the offline studying, the mental struggles, the times you're staring at the screen but your mind is elsewhere. They're like a skeleton of engagement without any of the flesh—just bare bones data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material and the course. It's not just showing up or clicking through things—it's being mentally present and invested. For me, real engagement would mean I'm excited to learn, not just anxious about grades. It's a state of mind more than a set of actions.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how my anxiety affected everything I did. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent worrying about the course instead of actually working on it. It also wouldn't show that despite everything, I was trying in my own way—just not in ways that translated to completed assignments or good grades. My effort was real even if my results weren't.
